title: Why Emotions Matter
body:
Emotions shape almost every important decision people make. They influence who we trust, what we avoid, which risks we take, and how we respond under pressure. If you cannot recognise what you are feeling, it becomes much harder to understand why you acted the way you did.

Emotional literacy matters for mental health. Anxiety, depression, trauma, and chronic stress all involve emotion systems that are overloaded, suppressed, or misfiring. Learning to regulate emotion does not mean switching it off. It means responding to it skillfully instead of being dragged around by it.

Emotions also shape relationships and culture. They drive attachment, conflict, empathy, loyalty, resentment, and forgiveness. At a social level, shared emotional reactions can fuel movements, panics, and political polarisation. Understanding emotions is therefore not just personal. It is social and civic.
keyPoints: Emotions influence decisions in work, relationships, and health
Emotional literacy improves self-awareness and communication
Emotion regulation is central to many therapies
Mental health often involves emotion systems that are out of balance
Collective emotions can drive politics, markets, and culture
Understanding emotions helps people act with more clarity
furtherReading: 'The Language of Emotions' by Karla McLaren
'Attached' by Amir Levine and Rachel Heller
